what: how to change text in javascript
how: how to add a class to an element
how to change an image src
how to change css with js

1_beginner_explanation.summary: Once you have selected an element, you can change almost anything about it. You can change its text using `.innerText`. You can turn CSS classes on and off using `.classList.add()` and `.classList.remove()`. You can change attributes (like an image's source) using `.setAttribute()`. And you can even change inline CSS styles directly using `.style`.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: If the DOM element is an actor on a stage, modifying elements is like the director yelling commands: 'Change your script!' (`innerText`), 'Put on a red hat!' (`classList`), 'Hold this prop!' (`setAttribute`).

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: When you modify `.style.backgroundColor`, JS updates the element's inline styles, which triggers a 'Repaint' (updating colors). If you modify `.innerText`, it might change the size of the element, which triggers a 'Reflow' (recalculating the geometry of the entire page). Reflows are computationally expensive.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: DOM properties (like `img.src`) reflect the current memory state, while HTML attributes (`img.getAttribute('src')`) reflect the original text written in the HTML file.

3_real_world_example.use_case: Toggling a 'Dark Mode' theme and updating a profile image.

4_common_confusion.trap: Using `.innerHTML = userInput` to display text. If the user types `<script>hackMyAccount()</script>`, the browser will execute it! This is called an XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) attack.
4_common_confusion.fix: ALWAYS use `.innerText` or `.textContent` when displaying text from a user. Only use `.innerHTML` when you completely trust the source (like your own hardcoded strings).

6_edge_case.quirk: `innerText` only reads text that is currently visible on the screen. `textContent` reads the raw text from the DOM node, even if the element is hidden with `display: none`.
6_edge_case.code:
const p = document.querySelector('.hidden-text'); // display: none
console.log(p.innerText);   // "" (Empty, because it's hidden)
console.log(p.textContent); // "Secret message!" (Reads the raw DOM)
